paragraphs.text: Dear Sir/Madam, as the Digital Fairness Act has been brought to my attention, I wanted to say that I'm very pleased that that kind of law is getting worked on in the European Union. As such, I wanted to expend on a few more points that I think could be beneficial to it. - First, as most other comments are already talking about, the freedom game publishers have in our current society can be hurtful to the customer, as they can freely decide to stop the support of some platforms or video-games, and make it impossible to play it, even when the game is designed for single-player use, thus making a product the consumer paid for unusable. It would be appreciated if it was possible to add some clauses into the Digital Fairness Act in order to at least reduce those abuses. - Secondly, as of today, payment processing systems have a lot of power over consumers and companies. As such, recently, Visa/Mastercard has been pressuring vendor platforms as "Steam" or "ich.io", in order to force them to remove some content, such as adult games or horror games. I don't think payment processing actors should be allowed to have a say about the product the consumer chooses to buy, as long as the transaction is legal, and it would be important to force them bylaw to respect that rule, to prevent those kind of companies from overstepping boundaries they should keep. - Thirdly, recently, some websites has decided to put a banner when you try to access their content with an ad blocker, with two choices. To either disable the ad blocker and to accept ads and tracking, or to pay a monthly subscription to their service and to get charged to view content. While I totally understand that those websites needs to be profitable, the usage of an ad blocker online is now pretty much required for personal safety. As such, even the National Security Agency of the United States of America, advise to use an ad blocker, as it can be a source of tracking, malicious intents (malwares, unintentional downloads, tricking old people into buying overcharged subscription for an antivirus they don't need...), or even be the source of dark patterns you are talking about on your website. I think it should be a right to be able to use an ad blocker online, and not allowed for websites to force the user to disable it or have to pay to access the content. - Lastly, due to some recent laws, some social platforms has started to block some content, and ask for an Identity Document, or a facial scan in order to be able to see it. The problem with this can emerge from two different entities. First, the social platform can use those documents, information, or face scans, to identify individuals in a population. It could therefore intensify tracking, even when the individual wants to stay anonymous, or try to keep information from his private life from leaking (as an example, someone that don't want their employer to monitor their activity online). Because of those risks, it should be illegal for companies to ask for those information if it's not for a reason that is strictly needing these (like for online paperwork or government processes). Because of that, a solution has been raised that the government could have an automated service for platforms to connect to in order to verify the age of an individual without the social medias having to process sensitive information. This would induce another problem, as, if this were to exist, the government would be able to track the individual the same way as the platform could. It could cause abuses, as identification of online individuals, tracking of people with opinions not in accordance with the government (like political opposants or whistleblowers), and be the cause of huge censorship on the internet. I think neither the social platforms nor the governments should have the power to verify your identity, and, if needed, should use transparent means of verification that would make it unable to identify the individuals. Thank you.
